skill.name: image-to-video
skill.description: Animate any still image on RunComfy — this skill is a smart router that matches the user's intent to the right i2v model in the RunComfy catalog. Picks HappyHorse 1.0 I2V (Arena #1, native audio, identity preservation) for general animations, Wan 2.7 with `audio_url` for custom-voiceover lip-sync, or Seedance 2.0 Pro for multi-modal animation from image + reference video + reference audio. Bundles each model's documented prompting patterns so the caller gets sharper output without burning iterations on the wrong model. Calls `runcomfy run <vendor>/<model>/image-to-video` (or endpoint variant) through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "image to video", "image-to-video", "i2v", "animate image", "make this move", or any explicit ask to turn a still into video.
skill.summary: Smart router that selects the best image-to-video model for your animation needs, supporting portrait animation, lip-sync, and multi-modal composition.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: All 117 static findings are false positives. The skill is a markdown documentation file explaining how to use the RunComfy CLI for AI video generation. External command detections are example bash commands in fenced code blocks, not actual shell executions. Path traversal and filesystem detections are placeholder tokens and legitimate config paths. Network detections are service URLs, not data exfiltration. Security measures are properly documented with token storage at mode 0600, HTTPS transmission, and download size limits.

medium_findings.title: API Token Storage on Filesystem
medium_findings.description: The RunComfy CLI stores API tokens in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 permissions. While this is a legitimate pattern for CLI tools, it does involve filesystem access for credential storage.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Token storage in ~/.config is standard practice for CLI tools, and the skill documents proper permission settings (mode 0600) which mitigates unauthorized access risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service URLs
low_findings.description: Documentation contains hardcoded URLs to RunComfy service endpoints (runcomfy.com, model-api.runcomfy.net). These are legitimate service references, not data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs are legitimate RunComfy service endpoints documented for proper tool usage. No malicious exfiltration patterns detected.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Detection in Documentation
low_findings.description: The 91 'external_commands' detections are all in fenced bash code blocks (```bash) within the markdown file. These are example CLI commands in documentation, not actual shell executions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All detections are in markdown fenced code blocks showing example commands. The skill is documentation, not executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Detection on Placeholder Tokens
low_findings.description: Path traversal sequences were detected in <absolute/path> and similar placeholder tokens. These are documentation placeholders showing user where to supply their own paths, not actual path traversal vulnerabilities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Placeholder tokens like <absolute/path> are standard documentation patterns, not user input that could be exploited for path traversal.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' on patterns like '0600' (file permissions) and '1.0' (version numbers). These are false positives triggered by numeric patterns in documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Flagged patterns are version numbers (1.0, 2.0, 2.7) and file permissions (0600), not cryptographic algorithms. No weak crypto usage found.

content.user_title: Transform static images into AI-animated videos
content.value_statement: Creating videos from images requires selecting the right AI model for your use case. This skill intelligently routes your request to HappyHorse for portraits, Wan 2.7 for lip-sync, or Seedance for multi-modal animations.
actual_capabilities: Intent-based model selection across HappyHorse 1.0 I2V, Wan 2.7, and Seedance 2.0 Pro
Portrait animation with identity preservation using HappyHorse 1.0 I2V
Custom voiceover lip-sync using Wan 2.7 with audio_url parameter
Multi-modal animation combining image, reference video, and reference audio
Native audio generation for animations on HappyHorse and Seedance
Model-specific prompting guidance for optimal output quality
limitations: Single-model routing per call; multi-model blending requires separate calls
HappyHorse limited to 15s video with output aspect matching input aspect
Audio files must be 3-30 seconds and under 15MB for lip-sync features

use_cases.title: Animate product photos for e-commerce
use_cases.description: Bring product images to life with smooth camera movements and subtle animations. HappyHorse preserves product geometry and packaging while adding cinematic motion.

use_cases.title: Create talking-head videos with custom voiceovers
use_cases.description: Generate spokesperson videos with lip-sync to your pre-recorded audio tracks. Perfect for localized marketing content with voice talent in any language.

use_cases.title: Produce brand-consistent narrative videos
use_cases.description: Combine a character image, scene from reference video, and voice from reference audio into a unified clip. Maintains identity consistency across brand assets.

prompt_templates.title: Simple portrait animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this portrait image with gentle camera movement around the subject's face, subtle breathing motion, and soft natural lighting.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user says 'animate this photo' or 'make this move'

prompt_templates.title: Product showcase animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 360-style reveal animation for this product shot with smooth orbital camera movement, maintaining packaging and branding integrity.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants product demo or showcase content

prompt_templates.title: Voiceover lip-sync video
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a spokesperson clip for 9:16 format that syncs lip movements to this audio track: [attach audio], with medium close-up framing and warm lighting.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides a voiceover file and wants it synced to video

prompt_templates.title: Multi-modal brand video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a branded narrative clip using [subject image], [scene video reference], and [voice audio reference]. Subject from image 1 walks through the scene from video 1 with voice from audio 1.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to combine multiple reference materials into one video

output_examples.input: Animate this portrait photo with a slow dolly-in camera movement
output_examples.output: A 5-second video clip of the portrait with smooth camera push-in, subtle facial expression changes, identity-preserved features, and soft natural lighting evolution.

output_examples.input: Create a talking-head video synced to this voiceover [attached audio file]
output_examples.output: A 12-second vertical video of a spokesperson with precise lip-sync to the provided audio, medium close-up framing, and professional studio lighting.

output_examples.input: Make a brand video using this character image, this scene clip, and this voice sample
output_examples.output: An 8-second video where the character from the image performs actions from the scene reference while speaking with the voice from the audio reference, maintaining visual consistency.

best_practices: Lead prompts with motion verbs: drift, orbit, dolly in, reveal, blink. Front-load what is moving in the scene.
Match duration to audio length when using lip-sync. The video will be silent past the audio duration if the clip is longer.
Use explicit preservation keywords: identity-stable, packaging unchanged, background geometry stable when you need specific elements to remain consistent.
anti_patterns: Do not restate the image content in prompts; the AI model sees the image. Focus tokens on what should change or animate.
Do not request HappyHorse animation plus Wan-style lip-sync in a single call; these require separate model calls.
Do not mix radically different aesthetics between reference image and reference video; output will drift from intended style.

faq.question: Which model should I use for portrait animation?
faq.answer: HappyHorse 1.0 I2V is recommended for portrait animation as it ranks #1 on the Artificial Analysis Arena and excels at facial fidelity and identity preservation.

faq.question: How do I add custom voiceover to a video?
faq.answer: Use Wan 2.7 with the audio_url parameter. Provide a WAV or MP3 file between 3-30 seconds and under 15MB. The model will drive lip-sync to match your audio.

faq.question: Can I combine an image, reference video, and audio in one video?
faq.answer: Yes, use Seedance 2.0 Pro which accepts up to 9 image references, 3 video references (2-15s each), and 3 audio references for multi-modal composition.

faq.question: What is the maximum video duration?
faq.answer: All models have a 15-second maximum duration. For longer content, you will need to generate multiple clips and stitch them together.

faq.question: How do I ensure my brand logo stays consistent?
faq.answer: Use preservation keywords in your prompt such as branding unchanged, logo intact, or packaging stable. HappyHorse and Seedance both respond well to explicit preservation goals.

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: JPEG, JPG, PNG, and WEBP formats are supported. Images must be at least 300 pixels in dimension, under 10MB, and have an aspect ratio between 1:2.5 and 2.5:1.
